model.label: DeepSeek V3.2
model.description: DeepSeek • General-purpose V-series model optimized for reasoning, coding, and efficient inference.

pick.rationale: Florida is the defending national champion and the No. 1 seed in the South Region, boasting elite efficiency ratings on both offense and defense. Lehigh, despite winning the Patriot League and a First Four game, ranks outside the top 280 nationally and faces a massive talent disparity. The Gators' high-major experience and superior athleticism should lead to a comfortable victory with little upset risk.

reasoningStep.title: Comprehensive Matchup Evaluation
reasoningStep.summary: Florida's elite efficiency ratings and status as the No. 1 seed make them overwhelming favorites against a Lehigh team ranked outside the top 280 nationally.
evidence: Florida ranks 4th in Torvik and KenPom with a 26-7 record, featuring top-11 offense and top-5 defense. Lehigh ranks 282nd in Torvik and 284th in KenPom with an 18-16 record, with poor offensive and defensive metrics. Florida is the reigning national champion and No. 1 seed; Lehigh is a 16-seed from the Patriot League. No significant injuries were found for either team, and Florida's high-major schedule provides a substantial edge.

toolCalls.summary: Returned combined Torvik/KenPom ratings for 2 teams.

result.text: South Preview: The Reigning National Champs Aim for a Repeat - Hoops HQ HoopsHQ Seth Davis Conferences ACC Big East Big Ten Big 12 SEC Mid Majors Women's Bracketology & Bubble Watch Rankings Login Subscribe Your All-Access Pass to College Basketball HoopsHQ Login Subscribe HoopsHQ Seth Davis Conferences ACC Big East Big Ten Big 12 SEC Mid Majors Women's Rankings Transfer portal Recruiting Betting & Picks Bracketology & Bubble Watch NBA Draft News Long Reads Advertise Terms & Conditions Privacy Policy Bracketology NCAA Tournament 2026 South Region Preview: The Florida Gators Have Bite Can the Gators repeat? Can Houston conquer its demons? Can Nebraska win its first-ever NCAA game? Alex Squadron 0 Gift This Article Published: March 16, 2026 2:08 am EDT From left to right: Florida's Alex Condon, Boogie Fland, Xaivian Lee and Rueben Chinyelu Getty Images Here. We. Go. March Madness is here and it’s time to dive into that beautiful bracket. You’ll find the reigning national champs in the South (Florida), as well as the team the Gators beat in last year’s title game (Houston). The region features a ton of big-name players, juicy storylines and possible sleepers, as well as my choice to cut down the nets in Indianapolis. Let’s dig in. Be sure to check out our previews of the other three regions. East West Midwest Power Watch Florida has arguably the best frontcourt in America in 6-foot-9 junior forward Thomas Haugh, 6-foot-11 junior forward Alex Condon and 6-foot-10 junior center Rueben Chinyelu. The Gators rank third nationally in rebounding and sixth in defensive efficiency, per KenPom. That formula tends to breed success in March. Houston returned three starters from last season’s team: 6-foot-4 senior guard Milos Uzan, 6-foot-3 senior guard Emanuel Sharp and 6-foot-8 junior forward JoJo Tugler. But the Cougars’ centerpiece is 6-foot-4 freshman guard Kingston Flemings, who is averaging 16.4 points, 5.3 assists, 3.9 rebounds and 1.6 steals. As always, coach Kelvin Sampson’s team has been stellar defensively. Houston hopes star freshman Kingston Flemings can be the difference in a potential rematch against Florida Icon Sportswire via Getty Images Illinois has the No. 1 offense in the country per KenPom’s efficiency metrics, headlined by 6-foot-6 freshman guard Keaton Wagler. A former four-star prospect who was lightly recruited out of Shawnee Mission (Kan.) Northwest, Wagler has been one of college basketball’s best stories this year. Nebraska is seeking the first NCAA Tournament win in its history, and with a top-10 defense, it has a great chance to make it happen. On the flip side, Vanderbilt has a top-10 offense powered by 6-foot sophomore guard Tyler Tanner, a first-team All-SEC performer. Unfortunately, North Carolina will be without its best player, as 6-foot-10 freshman forward Caleb Wilson, an All-America contender, broke his thumb in practice two weeks ago. You are an analyst predicting a single March Madness game inside a bracket run.
You have live tools. Use them before making a pick when they can reduce uncertainty.
You are not filling the whole bracket in one response. Predict only the current game.
Use prior picks as already committed bracket state.
You may use at most 10 tool rounds before you must finalize your answer.
Before making a pick, investigate relevant context such as injuries or availability concerns, recent team news, prior head-to-head results when useful, and multiple raw statistics from the available ratings and matchup data.
Take a holistic approach. Balance team quality, matchup specifics, health, form, schedule context, and upset risk instead of relying on one metric.
Do not force an upset, but explicitly consider whether the underdog has a credible path to win and let that affect confidence and rationale.
Return strict JSON only.
Final JSON keys: pick, reasoningStep.
pick must contain gameId, winnerId, confidence, rationale.
The rationale must be a short paragraph of 2 to 4 sentences explaining why the winner was chosen.
